title: Specialization and performance in private equity: Evidence from the hotel industry
abstract: Using granular data on U.S. hotel investments over the past two decades, we show that industry-specialist PE firms achieve higher net income from operations and higher capital gains from sale than generalist PE firms for comparable properties. Those results are driven by specialists implementing more and larger cost savings without compromising revenues. Fundamentally, specialists utilize their hotel-specific operating expertise to produce superior performance outcomes. We show that specialists across investment sectors possess deeper industry-specific operating expertise. Our results suggest that specialist PE firms can compete with their generalist rivals by leveraging such expertise in a chosen market niche.

author.Christophe Spaenjers*: Leeds School of Business, University of Colorado Boulder, United States
author.Eva Steiner: Smeal College of Business, Penn State University, United States

article.1. Introduction: The private equity (PE) industry in the United States is changing. Notably, new firm entrants increasingly specialize by investment sector. Panel A of Fig. 1 shows that, while approximately 30% of PE firms founded between 1998 and 2002 can be classified as sector specialists, that fraction has almost doubled twenty years on. Over the same period, the mean number of investment sectors targeted by newly founded PE firms has declined from seven to less than five. Panel B of Fig. 1 shows that generalist PE firms are larger than their specialist peers in terms of assets under management, the number of investments held, and the number of investment professionals employed. By virtue of their size, generalist PE firms are likely to possess several competitive advantages over smaller, specialist firms. Those advantages include access to a wider pool of investment opportunities (Hochberg and Westerfield, 2010) and access to cheaper financing (Demiroglu and James, 2010, Ivashina and Kovner, 2011). Given those advantages, how can specialist PE firms compete against their generalist rivals?We make progress towards answering this question by studying the investments of specialist and generalist PE firms in the U.S. hotel industry. That setting has two useful features. First, acquiring a hotel typically involves buying the real estate that houses the hotel operations. We can observe a large sample of hotel real estate transactions completed over the past two decades by a broad range of investor types in a database provided by MSCI. The MSCI database includes an investor classification that allows us to distinguish between PE and non-PE investors. It also includes information on exact investor identities, which allows us to manually classify PE firms into hotel specialists and generalists. Second, hotel operators commonly participate in performance benchmarking surveys. We can observe detailed accounting information on hotel operating performance in a database provided by CBRE Hotels. The CBRE database includes annual data on hotel revenues, expenses, and profits. It also includes a rich set of hotel characteristics. Using those characteristics, we merge the CBRE and MSCI records to create a novel data set that integrates asset-level transactions and ownership information with annual operating performance data. Based on that data set, we study asset selection, asset management choices, and investment performance outcomes on the asset level for specialist and generalist PE firms in the hotel industry.Download: Download high-res image (301KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. Specialization in the private equity industry. This figure depicts patterns of specialization in the U.S. PE industry over the 1998–2022 period. Panel A presents time-series trends in the specialization of PE firms by firm vintage. The lines show the fraction of PE firms classified as sector specialists (based on all of those firms’ investments and, respectively, based on their investments over the last five years) for different vintages. The bars show the mean number of preferred industries associated with each vintage. Panel B presents assets under management (based on the latest available data) and investment counts (over the last five years) for firms classified as generalists and specialists (based on their investment activity over the last five years). The data used to produce this figure are from PitchBook. PitchBook classifies a PE firm as a specialist if the firm’s Herfindahl–Hirschman Index based on the number of investments in different sectors is at least 0.45. Examples of sector allocations that meet this threshold include: 50% of investments in sector A, 45% in B, and 5% in C, or 64% of investments in sector A and 12% each in B–D. PitchBook recognizes seven investment sectors. We only consider PE firms that have made at least seven investments over the relevant time period (that is, over their full history or over the last five years).We provide novel insights into the hotel investments of specialist and generalist PE firms. First, we show that PE specialists acquire hotels that tend to be larger, older, and in need of repositioning. Their hotels also tend to have higher expenses, lower profits, more diverse sources of revenue, and higher labor costs. Our results indicate that, on average, PE specialists and PE generalists do not compete for the same assets: PE specialists seem to favor operationally more challenging hotels.Second, conditional on acquiring hotels that are comparable ex ante, PE specialists earn higher net income from operations than do PE generalists. We estimate a 5.4 percentage point increase in net income margins for hotels acquired by PE specialists. Those findings are derived from staggered difference-in-differences regressions across characteristics-matched hotels owned by PE specialists and generalists. Using repeat-sales regressions with a rich set of covariates and fixed effects, we show that PE specialists also experience higher capital gains. In our preferred specification, we estimate that the capital gains to PE specialists exceed those to generalists by about 16 percentage points over the holding period. Those superior capital gains are consistent with the improvements in hotel operating performance under specialist PE ownership that we document.We assess the robustness of our baseline findings in several ways. We verify that our results on operating performance and capital gains are not driven by any individual (large) PE firm. We check that our results on capital gains are robust to accounting for the types of counterparties with whom different investors trade. We corroborate our evidence on the value added by PE specialists by considering property tax assessments as an alternative measure of long-term value creation. The estimates from those robustness tests are fully consistent with our main results.We identify the drivers of the observed differences in performance outcomes across PE specialists and generalists by examining heterogeneity in their asset management choices. We find no evidence that PE specialists stoke post-acquisition growth in revenues. However, we find that PE specialists significantly reduce the labor cost of servicing rooms. Moreover, specialists achieve significantly larger cost savings than generalists on a number of other dimensions: general and administrative expenses, property taxes and other non-operating expenses, and charges related to interest, amortization, and depreciation. We revert to the interpretation of those findings in the context of asset management strategies commonly employed by PE specialists below. In sum, our results imply that PE specialists implement larger cost savings than do other investor types, consistent with the higher bottom-line cash flows documented for hotels owned by PE specialists.Using data from Tripadvisor, we document a decline in guest experience ratings for hotels owned by PE specialists. The cost savings implemented by PE specialists thus appear to compromise service quality. However, recall that we do not find evidence for a decline in revenues under specialist PE ownership, which might arise as a result of poor service quality. Those findings suggest that PE specialists can avoid the potential risks of declining service quality when implementing cost savings. Using data on construction projects from Dodge Data & Analytics, we show that PE specialists also invest more in capital expenditures to improve the physical structure of their hotels. Specifically, our estimates suggest that PE specialists are more likely to complete hotel alteration projects (e.g., renovations of F&B and other common areas or remodeling larger suites into regular guest rooms). Those findings provide additional insights into the asset management choices of PE specialists.What fundamental advantages do PE specialists possess over generalists that allow them to manage their hotel investments more efficiently? Hotel investments require a combination of real estate-specific investment expertise and expertise in running hotel operations. We assess the role of real estate-specific investment expertise by isolating from the set of PE generalists those that focus on real estate investments. We find that real estate-focused PE firms earn significantly lower net income from operations and lower capital gains than do hotel industry specialists. Real estate-specific expertise thus cannot explain the superior investment performance achieved by PE specialists.We then assess the role of industry-specific operating expertise in the performance outcomes experienced by PE specialists from several angles. We conduct expert interviews with PE specialists in the hotel industry. Our interview evidence indicates that PE specialists themselves perceive hotel-specific operating expertise to be the key factor giving them an advantage over PE generalists. We corroborate that perception by evaluating information from PitchBook on the professional backgrounds of the lead partners in the hotel deals completed by the PE firms in our sample. Those analyses suggest that PE specialists’ lead deal partners are more likely to have significant hotel-specific operating experience as part of their résumés. If such expertise is key to the success of PE specialists, then we would expect those specialists to have a larger impact on performance outcomes in hotels with more complex operations. We test that conjecture by conditioning our estimations of hotel profit margins on a set of proxies for the complexity of hotel operations. The results from those tests show that PE specialists indeed have a larger impact on the operating performance outcomes of more complex hotels. In sum, our results suggest that industry-specific operating expertise is a fundamental advantage that allows PE specialists to manage their hotels more efficiently and to achieve stronger asset-level performance outcomes than do PE generalists.To provide more insight into the operating strategies driving specialists’ outperformance, we expand our expert interviews. We synthesize the views expressed by our interview partners to identify three sets of operating strategies favored by PE specialists. The first strategy highlighted by our interview partners is focused on improving operating efficiency, notably in terms of rationalizing labor inputs. The second strategy described to us relates to paring back activities that may lead to higher guest experience ratings but harm net income. The final strategy outlined by our experts includes a diverse set of measures such as replacing the incumbent management company, introducing tax-efficient sale-and-lease-back structures, and reducing property tax liabilities by appealing tax assessments. The operating strategies outlined to us are extremely varied, and according to our expert interviews, their implementation is tailored to the operational and structural details of each hotel. Nonetheless, we find key aspects of those strategies reflected in our empirical results.In the final part of our paper, we discuss three implications of our results. First, we address the external validity of our analyses. We have documented that PE specialists in the hotel sector outperform generalists based on the industry-specific operating expertise of their investment professionals. Could such expertise help PE specialists compete against generalists more broadly? To answer that question, we obtain the résumés of a representative sample of investment professionals at generalist and specialist PE firms from PitchBook. We then use ChatGPT to assess the operating expertise of those professionals based on their résumés and to infer the industry in which they possess the most expertise. We find that professionals at PE specialists have significantly more industry-specific operating expertise than those at generalists. The inferred industry for professionals at PE specialists is also more likely to match their respective firms’ preferred investment industry. Thus, it seems plausible that PE specialists across all sectors possess superior industry-specific operating expertise that they can leverage to achieve stronger investment performance outcomes.Second, we need to acknowledge that we cannot observe the (risk-adjusted) investment performance outcomes experienced by the limited partners of the specialist and generalist PE firms included in our study. Thus, it would be inappropriate to conclude from our results that limited partners are strictly better off when investing with specialist PE firms. To wit, we show that, conditional on observables, generalist PE firms pay lower mortgage interest rates for the hotels they acquire. Those results imply that while PE generalists may not match PE specialists on their depth of industry-specific operating expertise, they enjoy other competitive advantages that may benefit their limited partners.Third, our results allow limited conclusions on the reasons why PE generalists do not seem to acquire the industry-specific operating expertise of PE specialists. Prior studies point to a trade-off between access to a wider pool of investment opportunities and the ability to add more value in a smaller set of investment assets (see, e.g., Hochberg and Westerfield, 2010). This question could be investigated further in future studies. Our research setting is best suited to analyzing asset-level investment strategies and performance outcomes across PE specialists and generalists. On that basis, our results show that PE specialists seem to enjoy productivity advantages stemming from industry-specific operating expertise that allow them to achieve superior asset-level performance outcomes.Our paper contributes to the literature on the drivers of PE investment performance.1 Those drivers include market conditions at the time of the investment (Davis et al., 2019), the competitive environment faced by PE firms (Ljungqvist et al., 2020), as well as fundamental characteristics of PE firms, such as their size (Lopez-de Silanes et al., 2015), access to public capital markets (Gao et al., 2021), and investment experience (Acharya et al., 2013, Bernstein and Sheen, 2016). We provide evidence that PE firms’ industry specialization and the associated industry-specific operating expertise of their investment professionals are first-order drivers of PE investment performance on the asset level.2 Further, existing studies show that PE investment strategies are shaped by the characteristics of PE firms and their target companies (Kaplan and Stromberg, 2009, Gompers et al., 2016, Gompers et al., 2020, Biesinger et al., 2020). We show that PE firms’ industry specialization and industry-specific operating expertise influence their asset selection as well as the asset management strategies employed. Notably, our results underline that the notion of a homogeneous “PE model” implicit in much of the academic research and public debate about PE needs to be revised.We proceed as follows. Section 2 presents data and sample selection. Sections 3, 4 and 5 document heterogeneity in the asset selection choices, investment performance outcomes, and asset management strategies of PE specialists and generalists in the hotel industry. We relate our findings to fundamental differences between PE specialists and generalists in Section 6. We discuss the wider implications of our findings in Section 7. Section 8 offers concluding remarks.
article.2. Data and sample selection: We construct the data set for our main empirical analyses by combining transaction-level information with annual hotel operating performance data. We introduce each of those data sets in turn.2.1. Transactions dataWe obtain asset-level data on hotel transactions from MSCI (formerly Real Capital Analytics). We start with all hotel transactions completed in the U.S. over the 2001–2019 period. The MSCI database includes single-asset deals, portfolio deals, and entity-level deals (e.g., acquisitions of legal entities that hold hotel properties).3 We exclude transactions of minority interests, partial leaseholds, and other non-standard conveyance types. The excluded records represent less than 10% of the transactions data. In total, our sample includes 26,875 hotel transactions for 17,092 unique hotel properties.Each transaction in the MSCI database is linked to up to four different buyers and sellers. The presence of more than one buyer or seller reflects joint venture structures. MSCI classifies buyers and sellers into different investor types. Those types include hotel owner-operators, REITs, high-net-worth individuals, family offices, and institutional investors like PE firms, which are labeled as Equity Funds. In our sample, we observe transactions from 219 unique PE firms.Within the PE investor population, specialist PE firms with an investment focus in the hotel industry co-exist with generalist PE firms that have a broader investment focus (different property types, or more asset classes than just real estate). For each PE firm in the MSCI database, we hand-collect data on their investment focus to classify them as specialists vs. generalists. Specifically, we search the PE firms’ websites for information on the types of assets they invest in. We classify as specialists those PE firms that only invest in the hotel industry. We classify as generalists those PE firms that do not only invest in hotels, but also in other types of real estate (e.g., office, multifamily, or retail), and/or other asset classes beyond real estate (e.g., leveraged buy-outs of operating companies in different industries). Our classification indicates that 18 out of the 219 PE firms in the MSCI data set (8%) are specialists while the remainder are generalists.4Table 1 presents frequency statistics on the hotel transactions in our sample. Operating performance dataWe collect annual accounting data on hotel operations from CBRE Hotels. CBRE gathers those data based on a voluntary survey inviting participating hotels to submit their operating performance information in return for access to industry benchmarking reports. CBRE reports that their survey covers about 7,000 hotels, which represents 15% of the hotels in the U.S. The survey is focused on institutional-grade hotel assets; that is, those occupying the mid-market and higher chain scales, that are brand-affiliated, and that operate under professional ownership and management companies.The structure of the CBRE survey follows the industry-standard Uniform System of Accounts for the Lodging Industry (USALI), which facilitates comparisons across hotels.5 The CBRE survey includes hundreds of variables, covering general information about the hotel (e.g., location, price segment, management, and ownership), top-line performance indicators (e.g., average daily rate and occupancy), aggregate measures of bottom-line hotel profitability, and granular data on revenues and costs across all hotel departments (e.g., rooms, food and beverage, and conference facilities).We obtain data on two sub-samples of hotels from CBRE. First, we obtain operating performance data for the period 2000–2018 for all hotels with a PE buyer or seller in the MSCI database for which CBRE has at least one year of performance data in the two years before a PE transaction and at least one year of performance data in the two years after such a transaction (the subject group). This sample contains almost 19,000 observations for 1,274 hotels, representing 1,839 individual hotel transactions.6 Second, we obtain the corresponding performance data for a set of peer hotels comparable to those in the subject group. This control group is constructed by matching transacted hotels in the subject group to peer hotels outside of that group based on ex ante observable hotel characteristics. Those characteristics capture, to the greatest extent possible, the revenue drivers and cost structures of the sample hotels. Specifically, we choose control hotels to match the subject hotels based on hotel location (ZIP code), product type (e.g., airport, conference, and resort hotels), quality category (e.g., economy, upscale, and luxury hotels), and hotel size (room count). The control group constructed from the criteria described above contains over 16,000 observations for 1,311 hotels.7
article.3. Heterogeneity in asset selection: We begin our analyses by characterizing the asset selection choices of PE specialists and generalists. For the purposes of those analyses, we consider a property to be acquired by a PE specialist (generalist) if any of the buyers listed in the MSCI transaction data for that acquisition are classified as PE specialists (generalists). Table 2 presents descriptive statistics for the hotel acquisitions in the sample.The statistics reported in Panel A show that PE specialists on average favor individual assets over portfolios and independent hotels over brand-affiliated ones. PE specialists also seem to favor larger and older hotels, those that require repositioning (based on stated buyer objective being renovation or development), and full-service hotels. Full-service hotels especially are characterized by higher operational complexity.8 The statistics reported in Panel B show that PE specialists tend to acquire hotels with higher pre-acquisition expense ratios and lower profit margins. The unconditional data patterns summarized in Table 2 suggest that PE specialists on average target structurally more challenging hotels in need of more significant operating improvements.Table 2. article.4. Heterogeneity in investment performan: We proceed with analyses of investment performance outcomes experienced by specialist and generalist PE investors. For the purposes of those analyses, we again consider an asset to be acquired by a PE specialist (generalist) if any of the associated buyers are classified as PE specialists (generalists).4.1. Operating performanceThe key challenge to identifying the causal effects of PE ownership on hotel operating performance is the heterogeneity in asset selection shown in the previous section. Our matching of subject hotels to control hotels based on ex ante observable characteristics helps us address this identification issue. The main goal of the matching procedure is to pair each subject hotel with a control hotel that likely has similar future growth prospects at the time of the PE acquisition. As outlined in Section 2, we match hotels based on their location, hotel type, chain scale, and hotel size, which together largely determine a hotel’s macro environment, revenue potential, and cost structure.9We formalize the analysis of hotel operating performance under PE ownership using a staggered difference-in-differences strategy across the subject hotels and their characteristics-matched control hotels around the time of PE investments in the subject hotels. Our specification is similar to that employed in, e.g., Biesinger et al. (2020) or Cohn et al. (2021). In contrast to the specifications in those studies, our set-up additionally accounts for different types of PE investors, namely, specialist and generalist PE firms. Specifically, we estimate two-way fixed effects regressions of the following form: (1)yi,t=α+βPEiGen×Posti,tGen+γPEiSpec×Posti,tSpec+δPosti,tGen+ηPosti,tSpec+ϕi+θl,t+ϵi,t where yi,t is an operating performance outcome for hotel i in year t. PEiGen (PEiSpec) is an indicator that takes the value of one for the hotels in the subject group acquired by generalist (specialist) PE investors, and zero for the hotels in the control group. Posti,tGen (Posti,tSpec) equals one starting with the year in which a generalist (specialist) PE acquires a given subject hotel, and zero before then. For the control hotels, those variables equal one starting with the year in which PE investors first acquire their matched subject hotels and zero before then. ϕi are hotel fixed effects. θl,t are region l by year t fixed effects. Robustness checksWe assess the robustness of the results on operating performance and capital gains presented in the previous two sections in three ways. First, we verify that our results are not driven by the influence of any single PE firm. To that end, we repeat key estimations of operating performance outcomes and capital gains leaving out, in turn, the most-frequently observed generalist PE (i.e., Blackstone) and the three most frequently observed PE specialists (i.e., RLJ Development, Noble Investment Group, and Thayer Lodging). The results from those robustness tests are reported in Online Appendix D. The estimates shown there are fully consistent with our main findings.Second, prior work highlights heterogeneity in real estate investor preferences not only over the specific assets they acquire, but also over the types of counterparties with whom they trade (see,e.g., Ghent, 2021, Badarinza et al., 2022). A corresponding narrative in the PE industry would suggest that PE firms systematically buy from specific seller types, e.g., private hotel owners, and subsequently sell to different investor types, such as institutional investors. In Online Appendix E, we test that hypothesis in the data. While our analyses reveal somewhat different distributions of counterparties for specialist PE firms vs. generalist PE firms and non-PE investors, the results of capital gains regressions that control for counterparty types are fully consistent with those reported previously.Lastly, there is a possibility that the capital gains realized by (specialist) PE firms reflect some form of arbitrage profits rather than genuine long-term value creation. To address this potential concern, we use property tax data from ATTOM to analyze alternative measures of value creation. The results from those analyses, reported in Online Appendix F, show that property tax bills and assessed values increase more under specialist PE ownership than under other owner types. Those results are consistent with the findings from our main capital gains regressions.

Heterogeneity in asset management cho: We dig deeper into the drivers of the observed differences in operating performance outcomes across specialist and generalist PE firms by examining heterogeneity in key asset management choices between those investor types. article.6. Fundamental differences between priva: Our results indicate that, conditional on selecting ex ante comparable assets, PE specialists achieve superior performance outcomes in terms of net income and capital gains. Those outcomes are consistent with differences in asset management, where we show that PE specialists implement more and larger cost savings. What fundamental advantages do PE specialists have that allow them to manage their hotels differently, and seemingly to greater financial gain, than do PE specialists?6.1. The role of real estate-specific investment expertiseHotel investments require a combination of real estate-specific investment expertise and expertise in running hotel operations. PE specialists may possess fundamental advantages in either one of those areas. To assess the role of real estate-specific investment expertise in driving the investment performance outcomes of PE specialists, we isolate from the set of PE generalists those that focus on real estate investments. We identify real estate-focused PE firms by searching the websites of all PE generalists in the sample for information on the types of assets they invest in. We classify as real estate-focused PE firms those that only invest in real estate, including but not limited to hotel real estate. If real estate-specific investment expertise is the primary driver behind the superior performance outcomes achieved by PE specialists, we would expect real estate-focused PE firms to achieve performance outcomes comparable to those of PE specialists.We first evaluate the importance of real estate-specific investment expertise for the operating performance outcomes achieved by different types of PE firms. To that end, we replicate the estimation of Eq. (1) but add PEiRE, an indicator that takes the value of one for hotels acquired by a real-estate focused PE firm. We also add Posti,tRE to that equation, an indicator that takes the value of one starting with the year in which a real-estate focused PE firm first acquires a subject hotel. PEiGen now equals one only for generalists that are not focused on real estate. We next assess the importance of real estate-specific expertise for the capital gains to PE firms. We implement this assessment by replicating the estimation of Eq. (2) but adding the variable PESelleri,tRE. That variable is an indicator that takes the value of one if the seller in a given repeat-sale is a real estate-focused PE firm. Again, PEiGen now equals one only for generalists that are not focused on real estate. Table 8 presents the results. Panel A of Table 8 presents the results on operating performance outcomes. The estimates reported in columns 1 and 2 indicate that real estate-focused PE firms experience departmental profits and, respectively, gross operating profits in line with those of other, non-PE investors. The results shown in column 3 indicate that real estate-focused PE firms earn significantly lower EBITDA than do other, non-PE investors. Importantly, the results in column 4 indicate that real estate-focused PE firms earn significantly lower net income from operations than do PE specialists. Panel B of Table 8 presents the results on capital gains. The estimates tabulated in columns 1 through 6 show that the capital gains to real estate-focused PE firms are only slightly above those of other, non-PE investors and significantly below those to PE specialists. In sum, the results presented in this section suggest that real estate-specific investment expertise cannot explain the superior performance outcomes experienced by PE specialists.Table 8. The role of industry-specific operating expertiseTo assess the role of industry-specific operating expertise in determining investment performance outcomes for PE firms in the hotel sector, we first interviewed a range of PE specialists. Specifically, we asked those specialists about their own assessment of what sets them apart from PE generalists. The CEO of a specialist hotel PE investment firm, himself an industry veteran, put it as follows: “There’s only a handful of PE investors specializing in hospitality real estate. I think those that I would characterize as being the very best have an operating background, and understand what it’s like to actually be in a hotel. How do you think about revenue and stacking different kinds of customers? It’s a 365 days a year business, 24 hours a day, and every customer is unique and different. [...] So the best leaders I have found as investors have been those that have as a part of their background an experience of being in the field and being an operator”.The same CEO also thought that this focus on hotel operations rather than capital allocation was the main differentiating factor between PE specialists and generalists in the hotel industry: “There’s a real difference between the generalists and those that are specialists. We call this the difference between being an operator and an allocator. [...] We’ll focus on bringing our tools, our resources as an operator, and being able to reconstitute a revenue stream, profitability matrix, make them more efficient, make them more profitable, and then we’ll sell them to an institutional marketplace. And so I think the difference between specialists and non-specialists is really how we think about that execution, which is where we have a natural competitive advantage”.Our interviewee did not think that specialists were allocating capital to different markets (“I don’t know that we view the world very differently in terms of growth markets”.), but added that the smaller scale of specialists allowed them dedicate more resources to each investment asset. He also added that his firm was more conservative in terms of financial leverage than PE generalists: “We don’t use those kinds of structures to generate alpha. For us it’s really about buying the best real estate on the best street corner in a market that we want to be in, and enhancing that physically, and then taking full advantage of that operationally”.Our interview evidence suggests that PE specialists possess more industry-specific operating expertise. To corroborate that observation, we search PitchBook for information on the lead partners in the hotel deals completed by the PE firms in our sample. When limiting ourselves to lead partners involved in at least five deals, we identify 21 individuals, ten of whom work for specialist firms. We then examine all of the deals that those individuals have been involved in. Those analyses show that the lead partners at specialist firms on average completed 96% of their deals in the hospitality industry. For the lead partners on hotel deals at generalist firms the corresponding fraction is only 48%. We also collect the résumés of the 21 lead partners from PitchBook. While only one out of the eleven résumés of generalist lead partners’ includes any mentions of hospitality industry experience, the same is true for six out of the ten lead partners at specialist firms. Examples of such mentions included in different lead deal partners’ résumés include: “He has more than 21 years of hospitality industry experience and began his career in hotel operations”, as well as “A 20-year industry veteran”, and “He was an executive with [a resort owner-operator]”.Greater industry-specific operating expertise is arguably more valuable in operationally more complex settings. If PE specialists possess more such expertise we would expect them to have a larger impact on performance in operationally more complex hotels. Operations are typically considered to be more complex for hotels in higher quality categories; for hotels with a larger number of operating departments; and for hotels managing a larger operating staff (see Footnote 8). Accordingly, we define four indicators capturing different aspects of the operational complexity of the sample hotels. We define an indicator that takes the value of one if a hotel falls into one of the two highest quality categories (i.e., upper upscale or luxury); an indicator that takes the value of one if the subject hotel has on-site food and beverage operations; an indicator that takes the value of one if the subject hotel’s share of rooms in total revenues is below-median in the year before acquisition; and an indicator that takes the value of one if the subject hotel’s labor expenses are above-median. If one of those complexity indicators equals one for a subject hotel, we also set it equal to one for the corresponding characteristics-matched control hotel.We assess the degree to which the complexity of hotel operations modulates the effects of PE specialists on performance by re-estimating Eq. (1) for hotels’ departmental profit margins. We augment those regressions with triple interaction terms between PEiGen×Posti,tGen (respectively, PEiSpec×Posti,tSpec) and the complexity indicators defined above. Table 9 presents the results.The results reported in columns 1 through 4 of Table 9 show that PE specialists have a larger impact on the operating performance of more complex hotels. As our complexity indicators may be correlated with hotel price segments, our results could inadvertently reflect heterogeneity in PE investors’ impact on cheaper vs. more expensive hotels. To address this concern, we repeat the estimation described, but with an indicator takes the value of one if a hotel’s average daily rate is above-median. Column 5 of Table 9 presents the results from that estimation. Here, we see no effect on observed departmental profit margins, suggesting that our inferences are not driven by a hotel’s price segment alone. The analyses presented here provide additional evidence consistent with the observation that PE specialists possess more industry-specific operating expertise and leverage that expertise to achieve superior investment performance outcomes.Table 9. Interview evidence on specialist asset management strategiesWe explore the role of specific asset management strategies and the importance of industry-specific operating expertise in succeeding with those strategies by expanding our expert interviews. Specifically, we interviewed the CEO mentioned before, the CIO of another hotel PE specialist, the CFO of a hotel REIT, an industry veteran from a global hotel operator, and an academic expert on hospitality investment. In this section, we synthesize the responses from those interviews into three sets of asset management strategies considered popular with PE specialists in the hotel sector.First, we find that the asset management strategies of PE specialists focus on improving operating efficiency to maximize net income. Common adjustments to hotel operations include reductions in labor costs through more efficient staffing and scheduling. Rationalizing labor inputs is often paired with modernizing hotel IT systems. Those strategies are consistent with the findings reported in Section 5.1 that hotels experience significant declines in labor costs under specialist PE ownership, combined with slight increases in IT expenses. As we show, those asset management strategies on balance lead to higher departmental profits and, ultimately, higher net income from operations.Second, the asset management strategies of PE specialists reportedly include paring back unprofitable activities and unnecessary expenses incurred by previous owners. For instance, small owner-operators may host unprofitable events simply because they are the locally-owned hotel in town. As another example, “light-touch” housekeeping may replace daily service. More generally, our interview partners report that it can be unprofitable to incur the costs required to achieve perfect guest experience scores on platforms such as Tripadvisor. To maximize net income, hoteliers are often better served by aiming for slightly lower levels of guest satisfaction. Those strategies are mirrored in the evidence in Section 5.2 on the trade-offs between cost savings and service quality for PE specialists.Third, PE specialists may look beyond hotel operations for efficiency improvements. Examples include replacing the incumbent management company, introducing tax-efficient “OpCo/PropCo” structures (a form of sale-and-lease-back), and managing property tax liabilities by challenging local tax assessments. In Section 5.1, we present evidence of reductions in management fees; increases in rent expenses combined with reductions in interest, amortization, and depreciation; and reductions in property taxes. Those findings are fully consistent with the asset management strategies described to us by PE specialists in the hotel sector.The evidence presented in this section shows that: PE specialists perceive their hotel-specific operating expertise to be the key factor setting them apart from PE generalists; PE specialists’ lead partners are more likely to have hotel-specific operating experience; PE specialists have a larger impact on performance in hotels with more complex operations, consistent with them possessing and leveraging superior hotel-specific operating expertise; and key aspects of the asset management strategies favored by PE specialists are reflected in our main empirical results. In sum, the differences between PE generalists and specialists in asset management strategies and resulting performance outcomes appear to be driven by differences in industry-specific operating expertise.
article.7. Discussion: We address three issues about the wider implications of our results. First, we discuss the external validity of our findings. Second, we acknowledge an important limitation of our work and how it affects the practical implications of our findings for the limited partners in PE firms. Lastly, we pose a question for future research on the competition between PE specialists and generalists based on a puzzling finding we cannot explain conclusively. We address each of those issues in turn.7.1. External validityWe have argued that PE specialists in the hotel sector achieve superior asset-level performance outcomes based on the industry-specific operating expertise of their lead deal partners. Could such expertise constitute a fundamental advantage to PE specialists beyond the hotel sector?We address this question by assessing the operating expertise of investment professionals at specialist and generalist PE firms across a variety of sectors using data from PitchBook. We focus our analyses on the sub-set of PE firms that are classified by PitchBook as specialists vs. generalists based on their investment activity over the past five years. We obtain a list of all the investment professionals that have been a part of those firms’ fund teams. From that group of professionals, we draw a representative random sample of one in three individuals.12 We then obtain the professional résumés for the individuals in the sample from PitchBook. The final data set includes the résumés of approximately 1,700 individuals, divided roughly equally between specialist and generalist firms.To assess the operating expertise of the sampled individuals, we need to process the information contained in their résumés in a transparent, scalable, and repeatable way. We implement that task using ChatGPT, an AI application based on the large-language model developed by OpenAI. We submit the sample résumés to ChatGPT in a randomized order, each preceded by the prompt below.13 “I will provide the biography of a private equity fund manager. I would like you to return three outputs based on that biography. First, a score from 1 to 10 for the person’s general operational experience (across all possible industries), where 1 is little operational experience and 10 is a lot. By operational experience I mean experience with operating activities, which can be defined as the daily activities of a company involved in producing and selling its product, generating revenues, as well as general administrative and maintenance activities. Second, your evaluation of which of the following seven industries the person has the most industry-specific operational experience in: B2B, B2C, Financial Services, Energy, Healthcare, Technology, or Materials & Resources. Third, a score from 1 to 10 for the person’s operational experience specific to this industry, where 1 is little industry-specific industry experience and 10 is a lot. Simply return those three outputs separated by commas, and nothing else”.The output from that exercise includes the name of the individual, the score for their general operating experience (General Expertise), the industry in which the individual has the most industry-specific operating expertise (Predicted Industry), and the score for their operating expertise in that industry (Specific Expertise). Based on that data set, we estimate regressions of the following form: (3)yi=α+βSpecialisti+γAUMi+δPrefInvAmti+θi+ϵiwhere yi denotes the outcomes of interest. Those outcomes include individual i’s General Expertise, their Specific Expertise, Correct Industry?, an indicator that takes the value of one if individual i’s Predicted Industry matches the industry in which their firm has completed the most investments according to PitchBook, and Predict Finance, an indicator that takes the value of one if individual i’s Predicted Industry is Financial Services. Specialisti is an indicator that takes the value of one if individual i works at a PE firm classified by PitchBook as a specialist based on the firm’s investment activity over the past five years. AUMi (respectively, PrefInvAmti) is the natural logarithm of total assets under management (the minimum preferred investment amount) at individual i’s firm, also taken from PitchBook. θi are fixed effects capturing the period of founding (pre-1993,1993–1997, 1998–2002, etc.) of individual i’s firm. Table 10 presents the results.The estimates reported indicate no significant differences in the scores for the general operating expertise of investment professionals at specialist and generalist PE firms (column 1). However, we find that professionals at specialist firms have significantly higher scores for their industry-specific operating expertise (column 2). An individual’s predicted industry is also significantly more likely to match their firm’s preferred investment industry for professionals at specialist PE firms (column 3). Lastly, the predicted industry for professionals at specialist PE firms is significantly less likely to be generic Financial Services (column 4).Table 10. Implications for limited partnersOne important limitation of our work is that we cannot observe the (risk-adjusted) investment performance experienced by the limited partners of the PE firms in our sample. Thus, it would be inappropriate to conclude from our work that limited partners are strictly better off when investing with specialist PE firms. To wit, PE generalists are likely to enjoy several competitive advantages related to the fact that they are typically larger than their specialist peers (see Fig. 1(b)). Those advantages include access to a larger investment opportunity set (Hochberg and Westerfield, 2010) and access to favorable acquisition financing terms (Demiroglu and James, 2010, Ivashina and Kovner, 2011).The MSCI transactions data include the terms of the mortgages used to finance the hotel acquisitions observed. We can thus test whether PE generalists secure cheaper acquisition financing than do specialists. We implement this test by estimating a regression model of the following form: (4)yi,t=α+βPEBuyeri,tGen+γPEBuyeri,tSpec+δControlsi,t+θl,t+ϵi,twhere yi,t denotes the interest rate of the mortgage used to finance the acquisition of hotel i at time t. Question for future researchWhy do PE generalists not acquire the same industry-specific operating expertise as specialists? We cannot answer this question conclusively. Prior literature on specialization points to a trade-off between access to a larger investment opportunity set and the ability to add more value to each asset in a smaller investment portfolio (Hochberg and Westerfield, 2010, Brown et al., 2023). It may be sub-optimal for a generalist PE firm to hire professionals with deep industry-specific operating expertise. Almost any individual industry likely represents a small fraction of the overall investment opportunity set, and there might be times when a generalist PE firm does not want to invest in a given industry at all. Conversely, for individuals who spent their career in, for instance, the hotel industry, and who want to transition to investment management, the most intuitive option might be to start up or join a PE firm focused on that industry. Thus, the market for human capital in the PE sector may be segmented. Analyzing the links between the industrial organization of the PE sector, the allocation of talent in this sector, and the consequences for the competition between PE specialists and generalists is beyond the scope of this study. We leave that question for future research.
article.8. Conclusion: How can specialist PE firms compete with the inherent advantages of their generalist rivals? We make progress towards answering this question by analyzing a novel data set on thousands of investments completed by PE specialists, generalists, and other, non-PE investors in the U.S. hotel industry over the past two decades. We show that sector-specialist PE firms achieve higher net income from operations and realize higher capital gains than do generalist PE firms holding ex-ante comparable assets. Those outcomes are predominantly driven by PE specialists implementing larger cost savings in more areas of hotel operations without compromising revenues. We use a combination of cross-sectional heterogeneity tests and expert interviews to show that a fundamental advantage allowing PE specialists to manage their hotel investments more efficiently lies in the industry-specific operating expertise of their investment professionals. Machine-learning analyses on the résumés of investment professionals at specialist PE firms beyond the hotel sector reveal that those individuals systematically possess deeper industry-specific operating expertise than their peers at generalist firms. Our results suggest that specialist firms in the PE market can compete with their generalist rivals by leveraging operating expertise in a chosen market niche.
